EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to parts of the southern Nebraska panhandle with snowfall amounts from 5 to 7 inches reported over much of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the snow, sleet and freezing rain brought treacherous winter weather conditions to a section of south-central Nebraska.  In some areas, 1 to 2 inches of sleet fell.  Locations near the Kansas border from Franklin to Hebron were coated with over one-quarter inch of ice.

The first major widespread winter storm of the season dumped heavy snow across nearly all of south-central Nebraska.  Snowfall ranged from 6 to 14 inches.  The heaviest snow fell in a 60 to 80 mile wide band along a line from Minden, to Hastings, and then to York. Hastings reported 14 inches of snow.  Minden came in with 10 inches of snow.  York and Geneva measured 9 inches of snow.  Six inches was common at many other locations.  A mixture of freezing rain and sleet preceded the heavier snow, especially south and east of Hastings.  Most schools in the area postponed classes for at least one day.  Very cold temperatures of 5 to 15 degrees below zero settled across the area once the snow and ice ended.  A 74 year old woman driving back to Mankato, Kansas from Grand Island, Nebraska became lost in Clay county.  The woman was found in her care after it became stuck in a snowdrift near Harvard.  She was uninjured.

A low pressure system moved out into the Central Plains from the Rockies producing a long lived winter storm for western and central Nebraska.  Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 5 to 9 inches combined with winds from 20 to 30 miles per hour creating blowing and drifting snow and low visibilities.  Arctic air filled in after the system had passed producing wind chill values of 20 to 30 below zero.  Numerous indirect accidents were reported throughout the area with only minor injuries.

A light mixture of snow, freezing drizzle and sleet impacted south-central Nebraska over a two day period.  Snow amounts were generally in the 1 to 4 inch range.  The icy mixture produced some glazing on the roads and several car accidents were reported.  One two vehicle accident near Bruning in Thayer county resulted in 5 injuries (indirect).  One vehicle accidents near Deshler and Hebron resulted in two injuries (indirect) as well.

The first major widespread winter storm of the season dumped heavy snow across nearly all of south-central Nebraska.  Snowfall ranged from 6 to 14 inches.  The heaviest snow fell in a 60 to 80 mile wide band along a line from Minden, to Hastings, and then to York. Hastings reported 14 inches of snow.  Minden came in with 10 inches of snow.  York and Geneva measured 9 inches of snow.  Six inches was common at many other locations.  A mixture of freezing rain and sleet preceded the heavier snow, especially south and east of Hastings.  Most schools in the area postponed classes for at least one day.  Very cold temperatures of 5 to 15 degrees below zero settled across the area once the snow and ice ended.  A 74 year old woman driving back to Mankato, Kansas from Grand Island, Nebraska became lost in Clay county.  The woman was found in her care after it became stuck in a snowdrift near Harvard.  She was uninjured.

This storm dropped 8 to 14 inches of snow over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, with over a foot reported across the Omaha metro area.  In fact, 14.1 inches was recorded at Omaha Eppley from this storm, tying it for the 3rd heaviest snowstorm on record.  The storm came through in two main bursts, the first started late Tuesday afternoon 1/4/05 and continued to around dawn Wednesday.  The second bout of heavy snow fell from late Wednesday afternoon until around midnight that night.  Toward the end of the snow Wednesday evening wind chill values fell to 10 below to 25 below zero as brisk north winds combined with temperatures that eventually fell to zero or colder.  These bitterly cold wind chill values continued into early Thursday morning.The storm claimed 2 lives Tuesday night as a young couple became disoriented after leaving their stuck vehicle in a rural area southwest of Omaha.  Despite calls for help on their cell phone, they were unable to provide enough detail to be located before they collapsed and froze to death.The storm closed many schools across the region both Wednesday and Thursday.Besides the 14.1 inches recorded at Omaha Eppley, other heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included; 14.2 inches at the NWS in Valley, 12 inches in Fremont and Gretna, 11 inches at Uehling and 10 inches in David City, Raymond, Springfield, Weston, Plattsmouth, Bennington and Friend.  Heavier amounts in southwest Iowa included; 12 inches at Little Sioux and Underwood, 11 inches in Harlan and Logan and 10 inches at Oakland.

Toward the end of the snow Wednesday evening wind chill values fell to 10 below to 25 below zero as brisk north winds combined with temperatures that eventually fell to zero or colder.  These bitterly cold wind chill values continued into early Thursday morning.The storm claimed 2 lives Tuesday night as a young couple became disoriented after leaving their stuck vehicle in a rural area southwest of Omaha.  Despite calls for help on their cell phone, they were unable to provide enough detail to be located before they collapsed and froze to death.The storm closed many schools across the region both Wednesday and Thursday.Besides the 14.1 inches recorded at Omaha Eppley, other heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included; 14.2 inches at the NWS in Valley, 12 inches in Fremont and Gretna, 11 inches at Uehling and 10 inches in David City, Raymond, Springfield, Weston, Plattsmouth, Bennington and Friend.  Heavier amounts in southwest Iowa included; 12 inches at Little Sioux and Underwood, 11 inches in Harlan and Logan and 10 inches at Oakland.

A winter storm brought locally heavy snow to parts of extreme southwest Nebraska.  Dundy county observers reported 5-6 inches of snow, with 2-5 inch totals reported in Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  Snowfall began during the morning with accumulations reaching advisory/warning criteria during the late afternoon and evening hours.

A mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet changed over to snow during the morning hours on 2/6/05.  Snowfall of around 6 inches fell in a relatively narrow band from around Seward to near the NWS office in Valley which is in extreme west central Douglas county.  The snow was preceeded by ice accumulations that approached 1/4 inch, although tree damage or power line damage was minimal due to only moderate winds and temperatures that were around freezing during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged area of frontogenetical forcing created a 30 mile wide band of 6 to 12 inches of snow from extreme southeastern Brown County southeastward through Loup County.  Winds accompanying the snow created very low visibilities at times.

A strong low pressure system moved out of Eastern Colorado and into the Central Plains dumping significant snow. Keya Paha County received 6-8 inches of snow, Blaine County received 9-12 inches, and Eastern Cherry County received 12-16 inches. Breezy winds also created areas of blowing and drifting snow across roadways. A semi-trailer and truck rolled over in the Long Pine Hills due to the slick road conditions. No one was injuried in the accident. Other cars lost control on snow covered roads but there were no injuries.

Intense low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with high pressure building east out of the Rockies and provided a prolonged high wind event over northeast and east central Nebraska and portions of western Iowa.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 55 to a little over 60 mph were common throughout the area.  The strong winds caused sporadic tree and roof damage, and there were several reports across the area of trees uprooted and a few semi trucks were overturned.  One person was injured in Decatur Nebraska (northern Burt county) when a roof of a building under construction blew on top of his house causing substantial damage.Some of the higher gusts measured by AWOS or ASOS sites included...62 mph in Fremont at 110 pm, 60 mph at Norfolk at 247 pm, 59 mph in Albion at 1010 am, 59 mph at Columbus at 115 pm, 59 mph in Tekamah at 119 pm, and 55 mph in Omaha at 224 pm.

A strong low pressure system moved through Nebraska overnight with high winds affecting the area behind the passing cold front. Northwesterly winds of 35-45 mph with gusts to 60 mph caused low visibility due to blowing dust in isolated areas.  The low visibility caused a multi-vehicle pile-up on Interstate 80 about 6 miles west of North Platte.  The accident occurred at 2:05 pm and involved 5 semis and 3 cars. Three semis and 2 cars were engulfed in flames. The accident claimed 3 lives (indirect fatalities) and injured 4 (indirect). West bound lanes on Interstate 80 were closed for 4 hours and east bound lanes on Interstate 80 were closed for 10 hours.

Rain quickly changed to snow behind a cold front and resulted in a narrow band of heavy, wet snow.  The snow area was about 25 miles wide.  It stretched from between York and Geneva, to Lawrence and then to far eastern Franklin county.  Many locations within the band reported 6 to 8 inches of snow.  Lawrence reported 8 inches of snow.  Snowfall rates were up to 2 inches an hour at times.

Snow of 2 to 5 inches blanketed a section of south-central Nebraska during the day and numerous accidents were reported.  The most significant accident was on Interstate 80 between Aurora and Phillips where eight semi-trailers were involved in a pileup. The interstate was closed for over two hours.  The Nebraska State Patrol reported at least seven other accidents on the interstate between Grand Island and York, one resulted in injuries (indirect) to 3 people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved out of Eastern Colorado and into the Central Plains dumping significant snow. Keya Paha County received 6-8 inches of snow, Blaine County received 9-12 inches, and Eastern Cherry County received 12-16 inches. Breezy winds also created areas of blowing and drifting snow across roadways. A semi-trailer and truck rolled over in the Long Pine Hills due to the slick road conditions. No one was injuried in the accident. Other cars lost control on snow covered roads but there were no injuries.

Intense low pressure over the Great Lakes region combined with high pressure building east out of the Rockies and provided a prolonged high wind event over northeast and east central Nebraska and portions of western Iowa.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts of 55 to a little over 60 mph were common throughout the area.  The strong winds caused sporadic tree and roof damage, and there were several reports across the area of trees uprooted and a few semi trucks were overturned.  One person was injured in Decatur Nebraska (northern Burt county) when a roof of a building under construction blew on top of his house causing substantial damage.Some of the higher gusts measured by AWOS or ASOS sites included...62 mph in Fremont at 110 pm, 60 mph at Norfolk at 247 pm, 59 mph in Albion at 1010 am, 59 mph at Columbus at 115 pm, 59 mph in Tekamah at 119 pm, and 55 mph in Omaha at 224 pm.

Rain quickly changed to snow behind a cold front and resulted in a narrow band of heavy, wet snow.  The snow area was about 25 miles wide.  It stretched from between York and Geneva, to Lawrence and then to far eastern Franklin county.  Many locations within the band reported 6 to 8 inches of snow.  Lawrence reported 8 inches of snow.  Snowfall rates were up to 2 inches an hour at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 55 knots (64 mph) for about nine hours caused minor property damage over a section of south-central Nebraska.  The strongest winds were along and north of Interstate 80.  The automated weather stations at the York and Aurora airports recorded 64 mph and 63 mph wind gusts respectively.  Power was lost to about 700 customers in York for a time due to broken power lines.  A 60 mph wind gust was recorded at Grand Island and 59 mph wind gusts at Ord and Kearney.  Sustained winds of 39 knots (40 to 45 mph) were common throughout the day.

Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with gusts around 60 mph persisted from late morning until late afternoon. The winds caused tree damage with a few branches and smaller tree debris broken off. There was minor damage to buildings, mostly to shingles and gutters.

Sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with gusts around 60 mph persisted from late morning until late afternoon. The winds caused tree damage with a few branches and smaller tree debris broken off. There was minor damage to buildings, mostly to shingles and gutters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel size was reported just east and northeast of Lincoln.
Quarter size hail filled ditches 6 inches deep in town.

Hail up to walnut size caused damage to vehicles and trees in Nebraska City and several miles west through south of town.  The hail was accompanied by heavy rain that caused some street flooding in town.
Heavy rain that fell in less than 1 hour caused extensive street flooding in Nebraska City.
Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter caused some tree and vehicle damage in Lorton.  The hail piled up a foot deep in spots and snowplows were used to clear some roads in the area.
Lightning struck a farm house and damaged the phone lines as well as electrical wiring to the well pump.
Nickel to golfball-sized hail reported.

The tornado touched down in the Brushy Creek area southeast of Maywood uprooting and breaking numerous trees.  The tornado then moved northwest approximately a mile into a farmstead where it broke power lines, destroyed grain bins, and tossed irrigation pipe.  It then continued northwest about a mile uprooting and breaking trees before hitting another farmstead.  At this farmstead, it destroyed 2 barns and a hog confinement building.  As the tornado weakened, it circled around the farmstead and destroyed 2 grain bins, small sheds and severely damaged a machine shed.  Wooden 2x4 beams from a destroyed shed impaled the house.  Hail associated with the storm, broke out windows in the home.
County roads were closed due to water running over the roadway.
Numerous roads were closed due to high water.  A few roads were washed away along the edges.
The hail, combined with estimated wind gusts of 52kts, damaged roofs and siding on homes and produced moderate body damage to vehicles.
Thunderstorm winds broke off eight inch diameter tree limbs.
Law enforcement estimated thunderstorm winds gusted over 60 mph west of Beatrice.  The winds caused some tree damage near town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds developed at the surface resulting in sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts of 55 to 60 mph over SW Nebraska.

A wild night of weather became the first major severe weather event of the season in south central Nebraska. Severe thunderstorms were responsible for tornadoes, very large hail and flash flooding, mainly east of Columbus to Hastings line. Fillmore County was hardest hit. A tornado in the northwest corner of the county damaged a center pivot. The county Emergency Manager was injured by flying glass after baseball sized hail pummeled his vehicles' windshield. He was treated and released from the hospital that evening. The hail hammered much of the county over the course of 3 to 4 hours. Geneva sustained some of the more widespread property damage. Damage in Fillmore County totaled in the millions. In York County, a tornado was reported south of Lushton, but not damaged was noted. Drifts of golf ball size hail and heavy rain pounded York County as well. 	Flash flooding was reported in York and Polk counties. In York County, the underpass north of downtown was full of water and the Beaver Creek Trails on the west side of town flooded. In Polk County, there were several roads reported under water around 8 pm in eastern sections of the county. Heavy rain of 1 to 3 inches in eastern Nuckolls County caused damage to county roads around Ruskin and Hardy.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado in far northwest Fillmore County knocked over an irrigation center pivot system on its short path.
Golfball size hail caused damage to roofs, siding, windows, and gutters on homes in Imperial.  A few vehicles also sustained minor body damage.  The northwest section of town was the hardest hit.
Lightning struck and destroyed the 1948 Opheim-Booth building along Highway 2 in Thedford.  The building was being used to store a few computers and other merchandise for sale.
A trained weather spotter recorded a brief tornado touchdown in an open field near Highway 30 west of North Platte.
Storm chasers recorded a weak tornado touchdown briefly in an open field.
Hail covered the ground.

Scattered severe thunderstorms rumbled across a section of south central Nebraska, along and east of U.S. Highway 281 in the evening and overnight. Two inch in diameter hail was reported southwest of Chapman in Merrick County and golf ball size was reported by trained spotters at Phillips in Hamilton County.  One severe thunderstorm with golf ball size hail flattened a one foot high alfalfa field north of Wolbach. The hail covered the ground, and literally pulverized the alfalfa right down to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

Fourth of July fireworks were provided by a crop-damaging thunderstorm in southwest Dawson and northwest Gosper counties. The thunderstorm slipped south of Gothenburg, then across the Midway Lake area and finally weakened just west of Elwood in northwest Gosper County. Significant crop damage was reported north and east of Eustis due to the wind driven quarter size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to three and a half inches of rain fell south of Guide Rock.
Hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating hail storm hit a small section of south central Nebraska. The storm developed east of Oxford, and then moved straight south between Stamford and Orleans in western Harlan county. Wind driven hail, as big as golf balls at times, ravaged corn, soybeans and unharvested wheat along a 10 to 15 mile path. Anywhere from 12,000 to 20,000 acres of crops were damaged or destroyed. In some cases, the soybeans were pummeled down to the dirt and the corn stalks were stripped clean. Monetary crop loss from this one storm will be over 6 million dollars. In general, it was too late in the season to re-plant.

Though well predicted, the severe weather and flooding on the night of May 11 was even worse than imagined. Thunderstorms ravaged a large part of south central Nebraska with hail, high winds, a tornado and catastrophic flooding.During evening and early morning hours, 4 to 12 inches of fell from Dawson County east to York County. At Kenesaw and Wood River, over 11 inches of rain was measured. The city of Grand Island set a rainfall record for a single event with 7.21 inches of rain. Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a severe thunderstorm knocked over or caused damage to about 20 tombstones at the Spring Creek Cemetery south of Ruskin. Several large trees were damaged or completely blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial "big" severe weather event for the 2005 season was quite a deal across south central Nebraska as several tornadoes were sighted. Of all the tornadoes, the most significant one occurred just east of Holdrege. The tornado set down south of Holdrege and bounced its way to the northeast side of town. About 5 miles south of Holdrege, two sheds were destroyed. This tornado damaged a hanger at Brewster Field and hit another farm just northeast of town. Five windows were blown out in the farm house along with other minor house damage. A barn was destroyed. Across the road, a center pivot irrigation system was heavily damaged. As the evening continued, there were numerous other tornado reports from Holdrege to east of Kearney. In Buffalo County, several twisters were sighted. One tornado displaced a barn onto a garage at a farm northwest of Gibbon. Another tornado kicked up quite a large dust cloud, but fortunately was in open county as it passed well north of Shelton. In Hall County, yet another tornado set down north of Wood River, but again causing no damage. The severe thunderstorms brought the usual entourage of hail, some wind and a little rain. Hail to the size of baseballs was reported in Kearney and there was vehicle and home damage scattered throughout the city. Golf ball size hail was reported at Oxford. Moth ball size hail was reported at Loomis and two inch in diameter size hail was reported near Palmer.

The supercell storm that evolved into a bow echo in Cherry and Brown counties also moved across Keya Paha County, uprooting and breaking off large trees.  A hog shed was destroyed and several 1200 pound hay bales were moved from a field into a ditch.  Several outbuildings also received major damage.  The storm also moved across Rock, Boyd, and Holt counties.

Thunderstorm outflow winds of approximately 75 mph overturned 5 campers, capsized 2 boats, broke numerous large tree limbs, and caused severe damage to 2 sailboats.  There were 3 people injured by the winds.  One person was injured when a tree limb fell and cut a woman's leg.  Another female was injured when a tree limb fell on her head causing head injuries and a broken collar bone.  Finally, a male was injured when a sailboat tipped over on shore and the mast hit the man's head.

A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly across the southern part of town near the Sarpy county border.
Hail up to around 1 inch in diameter fell from Bellevue toward Offutt AFB.  This was from the cluster of storms that dropped a little larger hail just to the west in Papillion.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was measured at the NWS office near Valley.
A NWS cooperative observer, KSDZ radio station 3 miles west of Gordon, measured a wind gust of 58 knots (67 mph).  Law enforcement reported several large tree branches down in Gordon.
Over 2 inches of rain fell in 45 minutes to produce lowland flooding with at least one road washed out.
Hail stripped leaves off corn stalks in a garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating hail storm hit a small section of south central Nebraska. The storm developed east of Oxford, and then moved straight south between Stamford and Orleans in western Harlan county. Wind driven hail, as big as golf balls at times, ravaged corn, soybeans and unharvested wheat along a 10 to 15 mile path. Anywhere from 12,000 to 20,000 acres of crops were damaged or destroyed. In some cases, the soybeans were pummeled down to the dirt and the corn stalks were stripped clean. Monetary crop loss from this one storm will be over 6 million dollars. In general, it was too late in the season to re-plant.

Fourth of July fireworks were provided by a crop-damaging thunderstorm in southwest Dawson and northwest Gosper counties. The thunderstorm slipped south of Gothenburg, then across the Midway Lake area and finally weakened just west of Elwood in northwest Gosper County. Significant crop damage was reported north and east of Eustis due to the wind driven quarter size hail.
Isolated severe thunderstorms brought a combination of small hail and strong winds to some parts of south central Nebraska. The hail and wind proved devastating for some corn crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts to 60 mph accompanied the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong winds and large hail caused significant crop damage over parts of Polk and Merrick Counties. Crop damage was also reported north of Arcadia and south of Upland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Not only did large hail fall, but 60 mph winds were noted as well. Six inch diameter tree limbs were broken.

Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

Hail knocked the windows out of building in Dannebrog.

Heavy rain that fell Wednesday night 5/11/05 caused Wahoo Creek and it's tributaries to flood Thursday.  The flooding closed several county roads, including briefly closing Highway 77 east of Wahoo.  Flood waters also caused significant damage to the Wahoo Wastewater Treatment Plant and also overspread the fairgrounds and stockyards near town. Wahoo Creek at Ithaca reached 20.4 feet at 1115 pm cdt on Thursday the 12th, flood stage is 19 feet.  The creek at Ithaca first reached flood stage at 445 pm Thursday afternoon and didn't drop below flood stage until 300 am Friday morning.  Heavier rain amounts for the 24 hours that ended at 7 am Thursday morning included 3.82 inches at Prague and 3.15 inches in Malmo.
Accompanied by 40 to 50 mph winds.
The hail covered the ground.
Mostly 1/2 inch or less.

Very heavy rain fell along the Lincoln Creek and Big Blue River basins in Seward county and locations upstream.  Rainfall of 3 to 5 inches was common over this area during the 24 hours ending on the morning of May 12th.  The rain caused Lincoln Creek, the Big Blue River and Plum Creek to flood near Seward.  Lincoln Creek reached 19.8 feet at 745 pm May 12th, flood stage is 15 feet.  The Big Blue River reached 19.5 feet at 1000 pm May 14th, flood stage is 18 feet.  Both the Creek and River dropped below flood stage by the 15th.  Farm fields and many county roads from around Staplehurst to near Seward were flooded along with some farm outbuildings and a few basements of farm homes. A few homes were completely surrounded by water for a time. It was estimated that the flooding caused around $140,000 worth of damage to county roads alone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell storm evolved into a bow echo producing very strong winds across northeast Cherry County, particularly in the town of Valentine.  The ASOS at the airport, on the south side of Valentine, recorded a wind gust of 69 kts (79 mph) at 8:44p.m. CDT, but a storm damage assessment estimated winds near 100 mph had hit the north side of town.  Winds tore off part of the Cherry County Courthouse roof, destroyed a 150ft section of the Hometown Lumberyard storage building, severely damaged the United Methodist Church, damaged 15 homes, produced roof damage to the high school, and uprooted or broke off numerous large trees.  The bow echo continued to move east and produced damage across Boyd, Keya Paha, Brown, Rock, and Holt counties.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported at 70th and Charles, 90th and Pacific and 108th and Polk Streets in Omaha.
Penny to nickel size hail was reported along with street flooding.

Rainfall of at least 3 to 5 inches fell overnight leading into May 12th over the upper reaches of the Big Blue River.  This caused both the West Fork of the Big Blue River and the Big Blue River to flood from around Dorchester through Crete.  The West Fork near Dorchester hit 21 feet at 7am on 5/13/05.  Flood stage is 15 feet.  The Big Blue River reached 26.3 feet in Crete at 830 pm on 5/14/05, flood stage is 18 feet.  The flooding affected mainly agricultural lands in Saline county, but some county roads were flooded and the flood waters even briefly crossed a few streets in Crete.  Damage to roads was much less than across Seward county, with damage estimated at less than $2,500.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 mph downed trees near 156th Street between Highway 1 and Church Street.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 to 70 mph rolled across the Papillion and Bellevue area.  The strong winds were accompanied by hail up to and over 1 inch in diameter and caused scattered tree and building damage across the area including knocking down several large trees.  The winds also overturned some truck trailers near 72nd Street and Highway 370.  OPPD reported that 3000 customers lost their power from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in south central Nebraska dropped heavy rain, hail and produced strong winds. Of note, rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in Howard County caused some minor flooding. The Turkey Creek north of Dannebrog rose 13 feet, but remained below flood stage. The Oak Creek in Dannebrog rose significantly, but also remained within its banks. Some basement flooding was reported in Boelus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though well predicted, the severe weather and flooding on the night of May 11 was even worse than imagined. Thunderstorms ravaged a large part of south central Nebraska with hail, high winds, a tornado and catastrophic flooding.During evening and early morning hours, 4 to 12 inches of fell from Dawson County east to York County. At Kenesaw and Wood River, over 11 inches of rain was measured. The city of Grand Island set a rainfall record for a single event with 7.21 inches of rain. Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly 1-2 inch diameter hailstones, a few up to softball sized.
Brief touchdown over open field near Highway 25 and Highway 36.  No known damage.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew down large trees, downed power lines and did some building damage near Creighton.  The strong winds lasted 15 to 30 minutes.
Rainfall of several inches in an hour or two caused flash flooding near Bloomfield and around the Bazile Creek area.  The flooding covered a few roads in the area.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the Albion Awos site.
The hail stripped some corn in the area.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked down large tree branches and caused some roof damage around Wausa.

High winds in the wake of thunderstorms uprooted at least 2 trees in Pierce and caused other tree and building damage in town.  The strong winds lasted for around 2 hours and were estimated at 60 mph.
Nickel to quarter size hail.
Thunderstorm winds caused minor damage to the roof of a house, and shredded window well covers at the house.
High winds in the wake of departing thunderstorms uprooted trees and caused other tree and building damage in the Randolph area.  The winds were estimated to have reached around 60 mph at times.

Hail up to softball size fell in a narrow swath across the Omaha metro area.  Most hailstones in this swath in Douglas county were 2 inches or less in diameter, with the largest stones, around softball size, reported in extreme northern Sarpy county in Papillion.  The swath of 1 inch or larger hailstones was generally east of 72nd Street and stretched from just southwest of the Capehart area southwest of Offutt AFB, into Papillion then toward the Florence area.  The hail caused scattered house and car damage, but since the largest stones were relatively isolated and fell during light winds, damage was not serious according to the Omaha World Herald.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado skipped along track as it moved SSE over open country in western Cheyenne county, NE.Damage primarily limited to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southeast portion of Perkins County had two to five inches of rain fall in a short period of time causing ditches and tributaries to the Stinking Water Creek to overflow. Several roads six to eight miles south southeast of Madrid sustained significant damage from water flowing over them.  The moving water carried a stack of 1200 pound hay bales across Rangeline Road south of Madrid.
In the northwest portion of Keith County, two to three inches of heavy rain fell causing water to flow over several secondary roads. One road was completely washed out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near 156th and Blondo Streets.  The hail was accompanied by winds of at least 50 mph causing some hail drifts several inches deep.
The hail caused vehicle and some building/house damage in town.
Tornado over open country.

Tornado moved SSE across parts of eastern Banner county, remaining over open country.  Some power lines, power transmission towers and poles blown down as well as trees uprooted.  Damage to irrigation pivots also reported.
3/4 to 1 3/4 inch diameter hail reported.
3/4 to 1 inch diameter hail reported.

High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

Nickel to golfball size hail reported.
Some secondary roads reported under water.

A weak tornado from a supercell thunderstorm was on the ground for about 4 miles.  Several trained storm spotters and passersby on Interstate 80 saw the tornado.  No damage occurred as the tornado moved across open rangeland.
A supercell thunderstorm spawned a weak tornado that made a brief touchdown over open rangeland.
The tornado made a brief touchdown over open rangeland.
In the northwest portion of Brown County, two to four inches of heavy rainfall caused water to flow over several roadways between Johnstown and the Niobrara River.

In the southwest portion of Keya Paha County, two to four inches of heavy rainfall caused water to flow over several roadways. The road south of Norden, between Norden and the Niobrara River, was washed out.
4-5 inches of rainfall resulted in the closure of many county roads and washed out one bridge. Rush Creek overflowed its banks.
Weak tornado reported to have briefly touched down over open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph overturned a semi-truck in Nebraska City.

Two to five inches of rain fell in about two hours causing water to accumulate quickly and flow over roadways. Four miles of Dickens Road south of Hershey was closed due to water over the road. Parkway and Wagon Train Roads also had water over them. There was also three feet of water over Wild Rose and Antelope Roads.

In the northeast portion of Chase County, two to five inches of rain fell in a short time causing ditches and tributaries to the Stinking Water Creek to overflow. One road 17 miles northeast of Imperial was completely washed away, while several others were severely damaged and impassable.

The initial "big" severe weather event for the 2005 season was quite a deal across south central Nebraska as several tornadoes were sighted. Of all the tornadoes, the most significant one occurred just east of Holdrege. The tornado set down south of Holdrege and bounced its way to the northeast side of town. About 5 miles south of Holdrege, two sheds were destroyed. This tornado damaged a hanger at Brewster Field and hit another farm just northeast of town. Five windows were blown out in the farm house along with other minor house damage. A barn was destroyed. Across the road, a center pivot irrigation system was heavily damaged. As the evening continued, there were numerous other tornado reports from Holdrege to east of Kearney. In Buffalo County, several twisters were sighted. One tornado displaced a barn onto a garage at a farm northwest of Gibbon. Another tornado kicked up quite a large dust cloud, but fortunately was in open county as it passed well north of Shelton. In Hall County, yet another tornado set down north of Wood River, but again causing no damage. The severe thunderstorms brought the usual entourage of hail, some wind and a little rain. Hail to the size of baseballs was reported in Kearney and there was vehicle and home damage scattered throughout the city. Golf ball size hail was reported at Oxford. Moth ball size hail was reported at Loomis and two inch in diameter size hail was reported near Palmer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado briefly touched down in open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in south central Nebraska dropped heavy rain, hail and produced strong winds. Of note, rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in Howard County caused some minor flooding. The Turkey Creek north of Dannebrog rose 13 feet, but remained below flood stage. The Oak Creek in Dannebrog rose significantly, but also remained within its banks. Some basement flooding was reported in Boelus.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail.
Shingles blown off roof.
Trees and power lines down.
A slow moving thunderstorm dropped penny size hail near Carroll for almost 20 minutes.
Nickel size hail fell for around 15 minutes from north of Crofton into town.

A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to softball size fell in a narrow swath across the Omaha metro area.  Most hailstones in this swath in Douglas county were 2 inches or less in diameter, with the largest stones, around softball size, reported in extreme northern Sarpy county in Papillion.  The swath of 1 inch or larger hailstones was generally east of 72nd Street and stretched from just southwest of the Capehart area southwest of Offutt AFB, into Papillion then toward the Florence area.  The hail caused scattered house and car damage, but since the largest stones were relatively isolated and fell during light winds, damage was not serious according to the Omaha World Herald.
At the NWS office in Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail broke windshields and heavily damaged vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial "big" severe weather event for the 2005 season was quite a deal across south central Nebraska as several tornadoes were sighted. Of all the tornadoes, the most significant one occurred just east of Holdrege. The tornado set down south of Holdrege and bounced its way to the northeast side of town. About 5 miles south of Holdrege, two sheds were destroyed. This tornado damaged a hanger at Brewster Field and hit another farm just northeast of town. Five windows were blown out in the farm house along with other minor house damage. A barn was destroyed. Across the road, a center pivot irrigation system was heavily damaged. As the evening continued, there were numerous other tornado reports from Holdrege to east of Kearney. In Buffalo County, several twisters were sighted. One tornado displaced a barn onto a garage at a farm northwest of Gibbon. Another tornado kicked up quite a large dust cloud, but fortunately was in open county as it passed well north of Shelton. In Hall County, yet another tornado set down north of Wood River, but again causing no damage. The severe thunderstorms brought the usual entourage of hail, some wind and a little rain. Hail to the size of baseballs was reported in Kearney and there was vehicle and home damage scattered throughout the city. Golf ball size hail was reported at Oxford. Moth ball size hail was reported at Loomis and two inch in diameter size hail was reported near Palmer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 mph took down a few trees and upset playground equipment in town. The winds were preceeded by 1 inch hail.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew some large tree limbs onto a porch causing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over Highway 25 three miles south of Wallace.
The Department of Roads reported flash floods washed out culverts on Boxelder and Moran roads.
The Department of Roads closed Highway 23 between Moorefield and Farnum because of water flowing over it.
Up to 3 inches of rain fell on already saturated ground causing a road to be washed out between Arnold and Callaway.
Rainfall of around 3 inches in several hours across parts of Pierce county caused flash flooding around the Plainview area.  Standing water was still across a local baseball field the next morning.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph or greater that lasted for 10 or 15 minutes uprooted 50 to 60 trees southwest of Plainview and toppled an outbuilding.  Trees, power lines and signs were downed in Plainview as well, causing some building and vehicle damage throughout the area.

Heavy rain, unofficially up to 7 inches was reported over parts of Knox county, caused flash flooding near Niobrara, Verdel and Verdigre.  Several dams were washed out in these areas and some county roads were damaged.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph flipped and twisted a center pivot northwest of Verdigre.
A few 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs knocked down.
Strong gradient winds developed during the morning hours. Local mesonet site sustained winds of 43 mph gusting to 53 mph.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

Heavy rain caused flooding along Shell Creek in Madison and Platte counties. Doppler radar estimated that 5 to 10 inches may have fallen over part of the drainage basin Monday night/early Tuesday morning the 20th and 21st. The flooding extended from near Meadow Grove southeast through Platte Center which caused the closure of Highways 45 near Newman Grove and 91 west of Lindsay.

Heavy rain caused flooding along Shell Creek in Madison and Platte counties. Doppler radar estimated that 5 to 10 inches may have fallen over part of the drainage basin Monday night/early Tuesday morning the 20th and 21st. The flooding extended from near Meadow Grove southeast through Platte Center which caused the closure of Highways 45 near Newman Grove and 91 west of Lindsay.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph downed trees and caused some building damage from near Ellis toward the west side of Beatrice.  The winds were accompanied by very heavy rain which caused some minor street flooding in Beatrice.
Thunderstorm winds, which were estimated at between 60 and 80 mph, downed trees west of Bennet and caused some outbuilding damage.

Rainfall of 3 inches in a few hours flooded several county roads south of Tilden.  Doppler radar estimated that a small area in western Madison county, in the Battle Creek drainage basin, could have received up to 10 inches of rain from these storms.
A tornado from a supercell storm made a brief touchdown over open rangeland.  Several storm chasers recorded the tornado as it touched down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though well predicted, the severe weather and flooding on the night of May 11 was even worse than imagined. Thunderstorms ravaged a large part of south central Nebraska with hail, high winds, a tornado and catastrophic flooding.During evening and early morning hours, 4 to 12 inches of fell from Dawson County east to York County. At Kenesaw and Wood River, over 11 inches of rain was measured. The city of Grand Island set a rainfall record for a single event with 7.21 inches of rain. Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.
Baseball size hail was reported with a renegade thunderstorm just east of Farnam in southwest Dawson County. This storm pounded crops that were hit with hail earlier in the week.
Severe thunderstorms in Furnas County produced 60 mph winds in Arapahoe and Cambridge. Power poles were snapped and there were several reports of six to ten inch tree limbs broken.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled over south central Nebraska during the evening hours. The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell tornado was caught on video by storm chasers along the Platte River ridge line southeast of Gothenburg. There was no damage caused by this tornado.

The most intense storms were in Dawson County where 70 mph winds and golf ball size hail were reported. Storm chasers video taped a brief tornado on the southeast flank of the thunderstorm. The tornado occurred in a very rural area and no damage occurred. There were a few sporadic power outages in Phelps County as strong winds blew through the county. Minor street flooding was reported in Holdrege for a short time, and over 2 inches of rain fell in parts of western and southern Phelps County.

The initial "big" severe weather event for the 2005 season was quite a deal across south central Nebraska as several tornadoes were sighted. Of all the tornadoes, the most significant one occurred just east of Holdrege. The tornado set down south of Holdrege and bounced its way to the northeast side of town. About 5 miles south of Holdrege, two sheds were destroyed. This tornado damaged a hanger at Brewster Field and hit another farm just northeast of town. Five windows were blown out in the farm house along with other minor house damage. A barn was destroyed. Across the road, a center pivot irrigation system was heavily damaged. As the evening continued, there were numerous other tornado reports from Holdrege to east of Kearney. In Buffalo County, several twisters were sighted. One tornado displaced a barn onto a garage at a farm northwest of Gibbon. Another tornado kicked up quite a large dust cloud, but fortunately was in open county as it passed well north of Shelton. In Hall County, yet another tornado set down north of Wood River, but again causing no damage. The severe thunderstorms brought the usual entourage of hail, some wind and a little rain. Hail to the size of baseballs was reported in Kearney and there was vehicle and home damage scattered throughout the city. Golf ball size hail was reported at Oxford. Moth ball size hail was reported at Loomis and two inch in diameter size hail was reported near Palmer.
Isolated severe thunderstorms brought a combination of small hail and strong winds to some parts of south central Nebraska. The hail and wind proved devastating for some corn crops.
Golf ball size hail covered the ground just east of Benedict as a severe thunderstorm moved over the area.
Golf ball size hail covered the ground just east of Benedict as a severe thunderstorm moved over the area.

A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

Though well predicted, the severe weather and flooding on the night of May 11 was even worse than imagined. Thunderstorms ravaged a large part of south central Nebraska with hail, high winds, a tornado and catastrophic flooding.During evening and early morning hours, 4 to 12 inches of fell from Dawson County east to York County. At Kenesaw and Wood River, over 11 inches of rain was measured. The city of Grand Island set a rainfall record for a single event with 7.21 inches of rain. Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

Though well predicted, the severe weather and flooding on the night of May 11 was even worse than imagined. Thunderstorms ravaged a large part of south central Nebraska with hail, high winds, a tornado and catastrophic flooding.During evening and early morning hours, 4 to 12 inches of fell from Dawson County east to York County. At Kenesaw and Wood River, over 11 inches of rain was measured. The city of Grand Island set a rainfall record for a single event with 7.21 inches of rain. Statically, this event was a 100 year rain and flood event for the area. 	In Wood River, 12 people were evacuated due to rising water. It was estimated every structure in Wood River sustained some sort of storm damage and wave after wave of severe thunderstorms pounded the town. Twelve homes sustained severe damage. The Wood River crested at 9 feet in town and flooding most streets.  Thirty-six homes were evacuated in the city of Grand Island as flooding was rampant over the west and north part of the city. The city's sewer system handled about 75 million gallons of water, or about 6 times the normal amount during the storm. Many parts of the business and residential districts sustained flood damage as the Prairie, Silver and Moores Creeks flooded. The Wood River near Alda, which had been dry for three years, tied a record with a crest of 12.2 feet early on the 12th. Elsewhere, three miles of roads were washed out in Kearney County and 15 miles of roads were damaged in Merrick County. Ten bridges were damaged in Merrick County as well. In York County, emergency rescues, one by helicopter and one by air boat, were made to retrieve stranded people trapped by flood waters southeast of Waco and near McCool Junction. Water was over Highway 81 north of Fairmont at the height of the event. In Fillmore and York counties, 5 to 8 inches of rain fell. Further west, a section of Highway 40 was washed out near Miller. Flood damage was in the tens of millions of dollars, with 12 to 15 million dollars in damage in Hall County alone. 	As flooding rains soaked the region, large hail, high winds and one tornado pounded the area. Hastings was particularly hard hit as baseball size hail and high winds belted the city for about 20 minutes. Literally, thousands of vehicles and homes sustained damage, especially on the east side of town, including downtown. Damage was estimated at 40 million, and that may be conservative and insurance agencies processed claims. State Farm Insurance was expecting to process 3,000 claims alone. Thirty-three people left their homes for shelter provided by the city. In Wood River, a tornado clipped the south side of town. In Merrick County, two Union Pacific trains derailed due to high winds and a tower for KMMJ radio was blown down north of town. A communications tower was also destroyed in Buffalo County by high winds. There were widespread, though brief, power outages in the area. At one point, about 60 percent of the city of Lexington was without power. Twelve center pivot irrigation systems were damaged in Fillmore County. High winds destroyed a barn southeast of Gothenburg and grain binds south of Darr in Dawson County. Officials estimate, 2800 homes and businesses had damaged in Grand Island. Both Hall and Adams Counties were declared Federal Disasters Areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph were estimated by a trained spotter in Ralston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wild night of weather was sparked by a large super cell thunderstorm which traveled from northwest of Grand Island to north of York. Along its path, there were numerous reports of funnels, large hail, high winds and even a couple of tornadoes. The tornadoes were recorded on video and digital pictures near Aurora and Stromsburg just after sunset. No damage was noted in either case. Earlier in the evening near Grand Island, numerous funnels and rear flank downdraft induced near surface circulations occurred. Strong winds near the airport destroyed a construction trailer. Minor damage was reported southeast of town. Near Marquette in Hamilton County, straight line winds destroyed an empty grain bin. Near the origin of the storm, baseball size hail was reported near Centura High School north of Cairo. There were numerous reports of hail large than quarters and wind gusts over 60 mph with the storm. Finally, on the flanking line, baseball size hail pummeled a car north of Minden and there was some minor damage to the lumber yard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured at Eagle Airfield southwest of Blair.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph knocked down several 8 to 12 inch tree limbs which blocked a street in La Vista.
Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph by the public.
Near 210th and Maple.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms spread hail, strong winds and heavy across a section of south central Nebraska early this June morning. Wind gusts over 60 mph dropped a large part of a tree onto a car in St. Paul. Heavy rain of 3 to 4 inches left ditches and low lying areas full of water in the Dannebrog area. Hail knocked the windows out of building in Dannebrog.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Saddle Creek and Dodge Streets.
Hail up to nickel size fell for around 9 minutes 10 miles north of Fairbury.
Hail up to golf ball size was reported from around 6 miles west northwest of Davey to 6 miles east southeast of town.
Nickel size hail was reported along with 50 mph winds.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by home weather equipment.
Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph by a trained spotter.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 80 mph caused tree damage east of Syracuse.  The damage was initially reported to have been caused by a tornado, but a survey done by the emergency manager revealed that the damage was probably due to straight line winds.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter fell in Gretna and at least to 5 miles east northeast of town for around 20 minutes.  The hail caused some minor car damage.
Hail up to penny size fell south of Papillion for about 10 minutes.
Accompanied by wind gusts estimated at 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms ripped across a small section of southern Nebraska during the day. The hardest hit area was Red Cloud, where 80 mph winds caused considerable tree damage and minor structural damage across the town. Near Cambridge, golf ball size hail was reported for about five minutes by the Cooperative Observer just east of town. The day began with thunderstorms moving north along a warm front in the morning and producing dime to quarter size hail in a few spots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to 5 inches of rain caused localized flash flooding along Skull Creek near Linwood.  The flooding covered several square miles of pasture land, spread debris across a few county roads and briefly completely surrounded a farmstead near town.
From the second storm to hit within an hour.
Hail around penny size fell for 10 minutes near Ashland.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding across parts of Otoe county...mainly rural county roads around the Dunbar...Syracuse...Unadilla and Douglas areas.  A Weeping Water man driving a pickup truck was swept off of county road 62 in extreme southern Otoe county by flood waters.  He managed to free himself and swim to safety suffering only minor injuries.  Part of highway 67 just north of Dunbar was washed out and water was reported over a bridge near minimum maintenance County Road F and County Road 36.  Rainfall of 2 to 6 inches was reported across the county, with much of it falling between 4 and 5 pm.
Heavy rain flooded several county roads along Lincoln Creek and other tributaries of the Big Blue River. Rainfall of 3 to 4 inches likely fell over much of the county this night.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 70 mph snapped trees in half near Highway 2 and the Otoe spur intersection.  This event was a continuation of the damaging winds in the area that began a little before 400 pm local time and originally was reported as a tornado.
